source.title: Occurrence of Hippocampal Ripples is Associated with Activity Suppression in the Mediodorsal Thalamic Nucleus

confidence_rationale: Provides the only direct evidence for the sensory gating function of MECH-122: thalamic activity suppression during ripples reduces external sensory relay, protecting consolidation episodes from interference. The spindle-coupled vs spindle-uncoupled dissociation precisely maps to MECH-122's two functions. Rodent data from mediodorsal thalamus reduces confidence given the MECH-122 claim targets a thalamo-cortical mechanism not the MD specifically.
failure_signatures: Thalamic suppression occurs during spindle-UNCOUPLED ripples (not during spindle-coupled ripples), meaning sensory gating and content packaging are temporally dissociated -- if MECH-122 requires both functions in the same spindle burst, this paper shows they operate in distinct modes.

The mediodorsal thalamic nucleus (MD) is a prefrontal-relay thalamic nucleus, not the sensory relay thalamus (VPL/VPM). Generalising MD suppression to sensory gating of z_self and z_world encoders requires an additional step.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The mediodorsal thalamic nucleus (MD) firing rate transiently decreases around hippocampal ripples in freely behaving rats. During NREM sleep, MD suppression occurs around spindle-uncoupled ripples (where it reduces sensory interference and favors memory replay), but MD activity increases around spindle-coupled ripples (where it may contribute to information transfer reliability). These two modes suggest competitive interaction between thalamocortical processing and hippocampal-cortical replay.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-122 posits two functions: (1) content packaging (spindle burst windows z_theta delivery) and (2) sensory gating (spindle burst closes z_self/z_world encoder pathways during consolidation). Yang et al provide the biological basis for the gating function: thalamic suppression during ripples reduces sensory relay to cortex, protecting the ongoing replay from external interference. In REE, the sensory gating function is the spindle-equivalent closure of the z_self and z_world input pathways during offline_consolidation mode, so that new environmental observations do not interrupt or contaminate the E3 -> E1 content delivery. The two-mode dissociation (suppression for uncoupled, elevation for coupled) suggests the gating and packaging functions operate in distinct phases even within a single replay epoch.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The study examines mediodorsal thalamus (prefrontal relay) rather than sensory relay nuclei (VPL, VPM, LGN). For MECH-122's z_self/z_world sensory gating claim, the relevant thalamic suppression would need to occur in sensory relay nuclei, not MD. This is a meaningful anatomical gap. The rodent model may not fully generalise to the primate/human thalamic architecture underlying MECH-122's substrate.
mapping.source_context: Freely behaving Sprague-Dawley male rats, silicon probe recordings in hippocampus and mediodorsal thalamus, NREM sleep, spontaneous ripple events

confidence_components.notes: J Neurosci publication, in-vivo single-unit recording in behaving animals. Mapping fidelity reduced: (a) the mediodorsal thalamus is prefrontal-relay, not sensory-relay; (b) the gating happens during uncoupled ripples, not during spindle-coupled events as MECH-122 implies. Transfer risk elevated: rodent MD may not predict the sensory-relay thalamic suppression required by MECH-122.
